ontology.foodex2_description: Snacks, savoury — mixed grain-based snack with legumes and condiments (estimated analogous code for chaat-type preparations)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Papdi Chaat. A0CKW (savoury snacks) is the closest defensible analogous anchor. Exact code unavailable in FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Papdi Chaat or equivalent Indian chaat snack category.

preparation_method: no-cook assembly of chutneys and yogurt
allergens: gluten (wheat flour in papdi and sev)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Papdi Chaat weighs approximately 250 g and represents a typical street-food serving as consumed in North India.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Papdi Chaat is a composite street food not individually listed in ICMR-NIN tables. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN component data for wheat flour products, chickpeas, potatoes, and yogurt, weighted by typical recipe proportions. Values are consistent with the input enriched_json record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.9). Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values from the input were cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for analogous fried wheat snacks and yogurt; values retained as plausible. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available in ICMR-NIN for this composite; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 (Atkinson et al.) — analogous estimate for mixed chaat preparation
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Papdi Chaat as a composite dish. GI of 65 (medium) is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database values for its primary carbohydrate contributors: fried wheat crackers (GI ~70–72), boiled chickpeas (GI ~28–33), boiled potatoes (GI ~78), and plain yogurt (GI ~14–36). The weighted average across typical recipe proportions yields an estimated composite GI of approximately 65. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 45.0) / 100 = 29.3 g (based on 250 g serving providing ~45 g available carbohydrate). This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (260 mg per 100 g; ~650 mg per standard 250 g plate) from chaat masala, papdi, tamarind chutney, and sev
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Papdi Chaat due to its high sodium load (~650 mg per plate); if consumed, request reduced chaat masala and omit sev to lower sodium by an estimated 30–40%.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-to-high glycemic index (~65) combined with a high glycemic load per serving (~29 g) driven by refined wheat flour papdi, boiled potatoes, and added sugars in chutneys
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should treat Papdi Chaat as an occasional food only; portion size should not exceed 100–120 g, and tamarind chutney (high added sugar) should be minimised to reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (3.2 g per 100 g) and trans fat (0.2 g per 100 g) from deep-fried papdi and sev, combined with elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Papdi Chaat to small portions (≤100 g) and infrequent consumption, given the combined burden of saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium that may adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried components (papdi, sev) may contribute to pro-inflammatory dietary patterns; yogurt provides probiotics with potential anti-inflammatory benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Papdi Chaat occasionally but should be aware that the fried components may promote inflammation; choosing baked papdi variants and increasing the yogurt proportion may reduce inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Papdi Chaat is a popular North Indian street food snack assembled from multiple components: crunchy papdis (small deep-fried discs made from refined wheat flour), boiled and diced potatoes, boiled chickpeas (chana), generous spoonfuls of plain yogurt (dahi), tangy tamarind chutney (imli chutney), spicy green mint-coriander chutney (hari chutney), and a topping of sev (thin fried chickpea-flour noodles), chaat masala, and optionally pomegranate seeds. The dish is served cold or at room temperature and is characterised by a vivid interplay of textures — crisp, creamy, and soft — and flavours — sweet, sour, spicy, and savoury simultaneously. Nutritionally, a standard 250 g plate provides approximately 450 kcal, 12 g protein, 25 g fat, and 45 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is high (~650 mg per plate), making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease without modification. The estimated glycemic index of ~65 and glycemic load of ~29 per serving place it in the high-concern category for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is vegetarian and contains gluten (wheat) and dairy (yogurt) allergens. It is a culturally significant festive and everyday snack across North India and is widely available at street stalls, restaurants, and during celebrations such as Diwali and Holi.
common_user_queries: How many calories in papdi chaat?
What are the ingredients in papdi chaat?
How much sodium is in papdi chaat?
What is the glycemic index of papdi chaat?

confidence_reason: Papdi Chaat is a composite street food with no single standardised recipe. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN component data and are consistent with the input ai_generated record (confidence 0.9). The GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. No exact approved-source entry for this composite dish exists; hence confidence is medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Indian street food preparation is not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2024) or USDA FoodData Central as a composite entry. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the assembled dish is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this composite preparation. trans_fat_g value of 0.2 g per 100 g is retained from the input record; this is plausible for deep-fried wheat products using partially hydrogenated oils in traditional street-food preparation, consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for analogous fried snacks. Cholesterol value of 8.0 mg per 100 g is plausible given the yogurt content and is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for plain whole-milk yogurt. Portions in the input contained three duplicate entries for '1 serving (200g)'; deduplicated to one entry. The default serving size used is 250 g (1 plate) as this is the more commonly cited street-food portion. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as (65 × 45.0) / 100 = 29.3, where 45 g is the estimated available carbohydrate in a 250 g serving (18.0 g per 100 g × 2.5). Direct GI measurement of Papdi Chaat as a composite dish is recommended for clinical dietary guidance.
